ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for sliced fish hor fun; A00LC (rice noodle dish) is the closest defensible category match.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker-centre plate of sliced fish hor fun weighs approximately 400 g, comprising flat rice noodles, sliced fish, vegetables, and egg-thickened gravy.

nutrient_source.title: Sliced Fish Hor Fun — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Sliced Fish Hor Fun (cr_id F05060120), confidence 0.95. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice noodles (flat, cooked)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sliced fish hor fun as a composite dish. GI of 72 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate component: flat rice noodles (hor fun), which are documented at GI ~72 (high) in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed international GI tables (Atkinson et al., 2021, AJCN). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 48.84) / 100 ≈ 35.2, rounded to 35.3 for a 400 g serving (12.21 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 4 = 48.84 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 283 mg/100 g; a 400 g serving delivers approximately 1,132 mg sodium, representing over 49% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced oyster sauce and soy sauce when ordering, avoid adding condiments at the table, and limit portion size to control sodium intake from this dish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Flat rice noodles (hor fun) carry a high GI (~72); a standard 400 g serving delivers approximately 48.8 g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~35, which is high.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to half a plate, request less cornstarch-thickened gravy to reduce refined carbohydrate load, and pair the dish with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat at 7.34 g/100 g with estimated saturated fat ~1.5 g/100 g; sodium at 283 mg/100 g is a secondary cardiovascular risk factor; fish provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease may consume this dish in moderate portions, benefiting from the omega-3 content of the sliced fish, but should request reduced sauce to lower sodium and saturated fat exposure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sliced fish provides omega-3 fatty acids with anti-inflammatory properties; ginger in the dish contributes gingerol-based anti-inflammatory activity; saturated fat is low at ~1.5 g/100 g.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may include this dish as part of an anti-inflammatory diet, but should request reduced sauces to keep sodium below 300 mg/100 g and avoid nightshade-based condiments that may trigger individual flares.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sliced Fish Hor Fun (鱼片河粉) is a popular Singaporean Chinese hawker dish consisting of wide, flat rice noodles (hor fun) served in a silky, cornstarch-thickened egg-based gravy together with thinly sliced white fish fillets and leafy vegetables such as chye sim or kai lan. The dish is typically prepared by briefly poaching or wok-frying the noodles and fish in a savoury broth seasoned with oyster sauce, soy sauce, sesame oil, and ginger, then finishing with a beaten-egg slurry to create the characteristic smooth, glossy sauce. The texture is soft and slippery, with a mild umami flavour from the fish and sauce. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides 132 kcal, 4.22 g protein, 7.34 g fat, and 12.21 g carbohydrate, with 283 mg sodium. The flat rice noodles carry a high glycaemic index (~72), making portion control important for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The sliced fish contributes lean protein and omega-3 fatty acids, supporting cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory health. Sodium content is borderline moderate and warrants caution for hypertensive individuals. This dish is a staple lunch option at Singapore hawker centres and food courts, commonly ordered as a single-plate meal.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05060120) with a reported confidence of 0.95. This is the canonical primary source for Singaporean hawker dishes. All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches from this approved source.
notes: sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — this dish contains no added sugar and the natural sugar content is below detection threshold (confirmed zero). saturated_fat_g of 1.5 g/100 g is estimated from the total fat profile of a fish-and-egg dish; the HPB source does not separately report saturated fat — this value is an approximation and should be updated when HPB publishes a full fatty acid breakdown. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient, and moisture data for this composite hawker dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on flat rice noodles (hor fun) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. Cultural metadata corrected: input incorrectly classified this dish as 'western' with dietary tags 'vegetarian' and 'plant_based'; this dish is Singaporean Chinese, contains fish and egg, and is not vegetarian or plant-based.
